cards.title: HVLA Manipulation — Principles
cards._legacyHtml:
Definition: High Velocity, Low Amplitude thrust technique applied at the end range of joint motion to restore segmental mobility.
Mechanism: Breaking adhesions in synovial joints; stimulating joint mechanoreceptors (Type I–IV); inhibiting pain via neurophysiological pathways; releasing gas from synovial fluid (cavitation — audible 'pop').
Spinal HVLA — Setup rules:
  • Localise to one segment (slack taken out of all other segments)
  • Patient relaxed — technique fails with guarding
  • Thrust direction: along joint plane
  • Thrust speed: <100 ms
Always: Pre-screen with 5Ds test before cervical HVLA.

cards.title: Maitland Mobilisation — Clinical Application
cards._legacyHtml:
Pain-Resistance Diagram: Map P1 (pain onset), resistance (R1), end of range (R2).
Treatment selection:
  • P1 before R1 = pain-dominant → Grade I–II (work short of pain)
  • P1 at R1 = equal → Grade III (into resistance)
  • R1 before P1 = stiffness-dominant → Grade III–IV (deep into resistance)
Reassessment after each technique: Asterisk signs (comparable sign) — retest immediately after each mob to confirm improvement.
Cervical: Unilateral PA (C5/6 most common). Lumbar: Central PA (L4/5). Hip: Distraction + AP glide. Shoulder: Inferior glide (abduction restriction).

cards.title: MET (Muscle Energy Technique) — Full Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Conditions of application:
  • Muscle must be placed in stretch to its barrier (not through it)
  • Patient contraction: 20–25% maximum voluntary contraction
  • Duration: 7–10 seconds
  • Post-contraction relaxation: 2–3 sec
  • New barrier: therapist moves to new end-feel
  • Repeats: 3–5 cycles
Clinical Examples:
Hamstring: supine SLR to barrier → flex hip isometrically → relax → gain 5–10° per cycle.
Cervical sidebending: sit, stabilise shoulder, sidebend to barrier → patient pushes head against hand → relax → increase range.
Sacroiliac: posterior rotation MET.

cards.title: Myofascial Release — Techniques
cards._legacyHtml:
Counterstrain (Jones): Find tender point → position patient in comfort position → hold 90 sec → slowly return. Used for acute, fragile patients. Works via proprioceptive reset.
Rolfing / Structural Integration: 10-session progressive deep fascial manipulation to align body segments vertically. Addresses global postural patterns.
Self-MFR: Foam roller — thoracic spine, ITB, hip flexors. 30–60 sec sustained pressure per point. Patient performs daily.
Instrument-Assisted IASTM: Graston/Fasciq tools — mobilise scar tissue, fascial adhesions. 2–3 min per region. Pre-heat with hot pack. Post-treat with stretch + exercise.

cards.title: Trigger Point Therapy — Clinical Maps
cards._legacyHtml:
Head & Neck:
  • Upper Trapezius TrP1 → temporal headache, neck pain
  • SCM → retro-orbital pain, earache, dizziness
  • Suboccipitals → occipital headache, retro-orbital pain
Shoulder/Arm:
  • Infraspinatus → anterior shoulder + arm pain (mimics cardiac)
  • Subscapularis → posterior shoulder, inability to reach behind back
Lumbar/Hip:
  • QL → lateral LBP, hip pain
  • Piriformis → pseudo-sciatica, buttock pain
  • Gluteus medius → hip pain, limp
Treatment: Ischaemic compression 60–90 sec, spray and stretch, or dry needling.

cards.title: Spinal Manipulation — Contraindications
cards._legacyHtml:
Absolute Contraindications (DO NOT manipulate):
  • Spinal cord compression / myelopathy
  • Fracture / dislocation at target segment
  • Osteoporosis (severe — T-score <−2.5 + fragility fracture)
  • Active malignancy at segment
  • Cauda equina syndrome
  • Vertebrobasilar insufficiency (cervical HVLA)
  • Atlantoaxial instability (RA, Down syndrome)
  • Active infection (osteomyelitis, discitis)
Relative (assess carefully): Hypermobility, anticoagulation, disc prolapse with neurological deficit, undiagnosed osteoporosis.

cards.title: Pre-Manipulation Screening — 5Ds + 3Ns
cards._legacyHtml:
For cervical manipulation — screen vertebrobasilar insufficiency:
5Ds:
  • Dizziness (provoked by head movement)
  • Drop attacks (sudden falls without LOC)
  • Diplopia (double vision)
  • Dysphagia (swallowing difficulty)
  • Dysarthria (speech difficulty)
3Ns:
  • Nausea
  • Numbness (face or limbs)
  • Nystagmus
Positive screen: Any one present → DO NOT perform cervical HVLA. Refer for Doppler ultrasound of vertebral arteries. Use Grade I–II mobilisation only if needed.
